queries:
SELECT COALESCE(p.column_name, u.column_name) AS column_name,
                   p.ordinal_position, u.ordinal_position, p.data_type, u.data_type
            FROM (SELECT * FROM UAT_DB.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS
                  WHERE table_schema = 'BI_PRD' AND table_name = 'GENERAL_JOB_ROLE') p
            FULL JOIN (SELECT * FROM UAT_DB.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS
                  WHERE table_schema = 'BI' AND table_name = 'GENERAL_JOB_ROLE') u
              ON p.column_name = u.column_name
            WHERE p.column_name IS NULL OR u.column_name IS NULL
               OR p.ordinal_position != u.ordinal_position
               OR p.data_type != u.data_type
            ORDER BY COALESCE(p.ordinal_position, u.ordinal_position)

SELECT p.n, u.n, p.n - u.n
            FROM (SELECT COUNT(*) AS n FROM UAT_DB.BI_PRD.GENERAL_JOB_ROLE t  INNER JOIN UAT_DB.BI.TEMP_ACTIVE_TENANTS AS act ON t.client_name = act.client_name AND t.client_region = act.client_region) p,
                 (SELECT COUNT(*) AS n FROM UAT_DB.BI.GENERAL_JOB_ROLE t  INNER JOIN UAT_DB.BI.TEMP_ACTIVE_TENANTS AS act ON t.client_name = act.client_name AND t.client_region = act.client_region) u

SELECT COALESCE(p.client_name, u.client_name)   AS client_name,
                       COALESCE(p.client_region, u.client_region) AS client_region,
                       COALESCE(p.n, 0) AS prod_rows, COALESCE(u.n, 0) AS uat_rows,
                       COALESCE(p.n, 0) - COALESCE(u.n, 0) AS diff
                FROM (SELECT t.client_name, t.client_region, COUNT(*) AS n
                      FROM UAT_DB.BI_PRD.GENERAL_JOB_ROLE t  INNER JOIN UAT_DB.BI.TEMP_ACTIVE_TENANTS AS act ON t.client_name = act.client_name AND t.client_region = act.client_region
                      GROUP BY 1, 2) p
                FULL JOIN (SELECT t.client_name, t.client_region, COUNT(*) AS n
                      FROM UAT_DB.BI.GENERAL_JOB_ROLE t  INNER JOIN UAT_DB.BI.TEMP_ACTIVE_TENANTS AS act ON t.client_name = act.client_name AND t.client_region = act.client_region
                      GROUP BY 1, 2) u
                  ON p.client_name = u.client_name AND p.client_region = u.client_region
                WHERE COALESCE(p.n, 0) != COALESCE(u.n, 0)
                ORDER BY ABS(COALESCE(p.n, 0) - COALESCE(u.n, 0)) DESC
                LIMIT 1000

SELECT t."Job Role ID", t."CLIENT_NAME", t."CLIENT_REGION", COUNT(*) AS n
                FROM UAT_DB.BI_PRD.GENERAL_JOB_ROLE t  INNER JOIN UAT_DB.BI.TEMP_ACTIVE_TENANTS AS act ON t.client_name = act.client_name AND t.client_region = act.client_region
                GROUP BY ALL HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
                ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 1000

SELECT t."Job Role ID", t."CLIENT_NAME", t."CLIENT_REGION", COUNT(*) AS n
                FROM UAT_DB.BI.GENERAL_JOB_ROLE t  INNER JOIN UAT_DB.BI.TEMP_ACTIVE_TENANTS AS act ON t.client_name = act.client_name AND t.client_region = act.client_region
                GROUP BY ALL HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
                ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 1000

The the role of the Bishop, along with fulfilling the responsibilities of a Priest, is to:
Teach - A bishop is the principal teacher in his diocese and has a responsibility to preach the Word of God to his people. He must ensure that those delegated to teach in his name, namely priests, teachers, catechists and others, teach the truth.
Govern - This refers to meeting the needs of the local community (material, social, personal and spiritual) as well as ensuring that church laws are observed. He is ultimately responsible for training and supplying priests for parishes, for the finances of the diocese and for all church property. A bishop has the power to make church laws, be a judge in church matters and to enforce observance of these laws. These laws generally relate to worship, preaching, administration of the sacraments, safeguarding the faith and morals of the faithful and religious instruction.
Sanctify - A bishop is responsible for ensuring that the sacraments are administered and has the special authority to ordain priests and to confirm. It is usually the case, then, for a bishop to ordain the priests who are to serve in his diocese and to travel around the diocese and administer the Sacrament of Confirmation. He must also ensure that Mass is celebrated in the diocese every Sunday and on major feast day

As a Security Guard, you will be responsible for ensuring the safety and security of a specific location. You will monitor and control access to the location, respond to emergencies, and report any incidents to appropriate authorities. You will be required to maintain a high level of professionalism and customer service.

Observation skills: A good security guard should be observant and able to pay close attention to their surroundings to detect any potential threats or suspicious activity.
	Communication skills: Effective communication is essential in security, as guards need to be able to communicate clearly and quickly with other guards, law enforcement, and the public.
	Physical fitness: Security guards must be physically fit and able to stand, walk, and patrol for extended periods of time.
	Problem-solving skills: Security guards must be able to think quickly and respond effectively in emergency situations.
	Knowledge of security procedures: A good security guard should have a thorough understanding of security procedures, such as how to handle emergencies, check identification, and maintain secure access control.
	Attention to detail: Security guards must pay close attention to detail to detect potential security risks or breaches.
	Customer service skills: Security guards often interact with the public, so good customer service skills are essential.
	Ability to work independently and in a team: Security guards may work alone or in a team, so they need to be able to work independently as well as collaboratively with others.
	Conflict resolution skills: Security guards may need to resolve conflicts, such as disagreements between individuals or groups, so they should have good conflict resolution skills.
	Knowledge of technology: Security guards may need to operate security equipment, such as cameras or access control systems, so they should have a basic understanding of technology.

Role Purpose

Oversee the Apprenticeship Support Australia contracts and ensure their continuing compliance to KPIs, high level of customer service and other related activities which ensure the continued retention of the contracts. To be a key resource in preparing, reviewing, and submitting any related tender application both in preparation for and when the AASN contract is due for renewal. Success in this role would see CCIWA retaining the ASA contract whilst ASA increase both WA market share of commencements and in-training.
Identify new material growth opportunities for CCIWA with particular focus on education, skills, and employment activities. Success in this role would see a material improvement ($1m net profit pa over the next two years) to organisational sustainability and reduce CCIWAs dependency on the ASA contract whilst providing additional services which align to CCIWAs purpose.
Oversee the Work Integrated Learning, Educational Development Fund, Industry Training Hubs and any new programs brought on as part of the growth opportunities. Success in this role would see CCIWA achieve and exceed the requirements under the related programs.

Leading Culture  Engagement

Our leaders play a pivotal role in shaping and building a diverse culture that unities our people around CCIWAs purpose. You will create an environment of respect and inclusion where people can be themselves and feel safe to share ideas and experiences.
Instrumental in driving engagement, you will create department specific engagement initiatives aligned to CCIWAs broader engagement plan.
You will create strong relationships across the business to build collaboration, foster curiosity, and resilience to deliver CCIWAs strategic outcomes.
As a contemporary people leader, you will inspire with optimism and energy creating a learning environment where people strive for higher performance.

Key Responsibilities

Proactively seek out new material growth opportunities for CCIWA with particular focus on education, skills, and employment activities. Perform research and or business development activities to move these opportunities forwards. Action the related activities required to obtain the new work including internal business case justification/appropriate approvals, completion of client proposals/presentations/tenders/etc.
Proactively seek out and maintain senior relationships with key internal and external stakeholders which act to either strengthen existing relationships/revenue or to grow and develop new offerings.
Oversee and monitor:

Operational strategies to achieve and exceed contractual requirements and reporting requirements in a timely and effective manner
The growth of existing Portfolio market share and client base
Activity occurring across teams including improve processes and performance.


Provide leadership to the Portfolio of teams. Directly mentor and manage Portfolio leads to enhance their own performance and accountabilities as well as assist them to raise the performance and enhance culture of their respective teams. Oversee performance management and team culture initiatives across Portfolio.
Ensure strong communications across Portfolio on progress, achievements, and areas for improvement to build strong team culture with a key focus on ensuring all locations are engaged (such as Bishop See, Osborne Park, regional team, working from home).
Collaborate with Marketing and Communications team to co-develop strategic marketing, promotional and communications strategies to promote and grow Portfolio services and market share.
Directly manage the Education Services unit that implements projects and initiatives funded through CCIWAs Education Development Fund and ensure the work is appropriately communicated to those members who contribute.
Lead the Industry Training Hubs team and manage the Facilitator role(s) directly. Ensure that all contractual obligations are adhered to and that KPIs are met or exceeded.
Oversee the Work Integrated Learning Program and the delivery of the contractual KPIs.
Conduct appropriate levels of self-directed learning, particularly in relation to education, skills, and employment activities policy issues and provide input to CCIWA Advocacy as required.
Ensure development activities are conducted in relation to being on a Board including completing the required Director Professional Development (DPD) requirements to remain a member of the AICD and continue to sit as a member of the Apprenticeships Australia Pty Ltd Board providing governance and strategic direction to the entity.

Role purpose
The RAP Manager coordinates and supports the design, implementation, and monitoring of the AICD’s Reconciliation Action Plan (RAP). Reflecting the importance of the RAP to AICD’s reconciliation journey, the position sits within People and Culture and reports to the Chief People Officer. The role is responsible for coordinating actions across the RAP program, driving collaboration, good practice and urgency in progress on RAP deliverables, monitoring and reporting of RAP progress. Working closely with the AICD’s RAP Working Group, Executives and First Nations Sector Lead, the position will drive RAP coordination and support engagement with internal and external stakeholders relevant to AICD’s RAP objectives. 

Key responsibilities 
RAP Design, Coordination and Implementation
•	Lead coordination and internal collaboration on AICD’s Innovate RAP deliverables, supporting the RAP Working Group co-Chairs. 
•	Support the RAP Working Group through agendas, papers and progress reports.
•	Work with AICD’s project office on monitoring, reporting and risks across the RAP program, including relevant Executive Committee and Board reports and engagements.
•	Work with AICD’s People & Culture team on monitoring, reporting and risks with AICD’s cultural competency growth and internal change program.
•	Establish professional networks and relationships to support good practice and approaches
•	Role model AICD’s values as a project leader. 

Consultation and Partnerships
•	Lead liaison with Reconciliation Australia to ensure AICD is meeting RAP requirements and applying challenge and good practice approaches to support RAP implementation.
•	Working with the RAP Working Group, identify and support partnerships with like-minded RAP organisations relevant to AICD’s RAP deliverables and objectives.
•	Engage with the First Nations members on the RAP Working Group, AICD’s First Nations staff members and other stakeholders to ensure that RAP actions incorporate a diverse range of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander voices and perspectives. 

Internal Stakeholders and Communications
•	In consultation with relevant internal stakeholders, support the design and implementation of a RAP communications strategy to inform members, staff and volunteers of AICD’s RAP progress, aligned to the cultural competency uplift deliverables of the RAP.
•	Consult with RAP deliverable owners and the RAP Working Group to support effective collaboration across the RAP program, identify and escalate risks and resource constraints and monitor progress.
•	Engage with the RAP Working Group and Change Champions to drive cross-AICD coordination, prioritisation and progress on RAP deliverables.
•	Support the RAP Working Group co-Chairs in engagement with, and activation of, the MD & CEO and AICD Chair as RAP Champions. 
•	Collaborate with AICD’s Sector Lead – First Nations, on relevant sector initiatives and programs.

Reporting and Monitoring
•	Monitor RAP deliverables to ensure timeframes are met, activities are consistent with good practice and informed by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander inclusion and self-determination.
•	Prepare and submit regular reports on RAP program, with support from AICD’s project office.
•	Collaborate with the RAP Working Group to identify and respond to road-blocks, delays or risks.

AICD Contribution
•	Contribute to the development of the department operation plan, suggesting ideas that create solutions for the team, and in turn the organisation. 
•	Undertake or contribute to specific National and State projects or initiatives that support AICD’s strategic plan as required. 
•	Proactively contribute feedback and ideas to AICD for continuous improvement and to achieve OKRs, budgets and strategic goals. 

Other duties in line with competencies and skills may be required on an ad hoc or permanent basis

Essential knowledge, skills and experience
•	Experience in supporting development or delivery of a RAP, or related program.
•	Experience in supporting or driving cross-organisation projects and/or change programs. 
•	Strong project management and administrative skills.
•	Understanding of reconciliation and RAP frameworks.
•	Well-developed organisation skills including capacity to work in a matrix structure.
•	Highly developed interpersonal skills with ability to build strong collaborative relationships internally and externally.

Desirable 
•	Demonstrated experience in supporting delivery of a national organisation RAP.
•	Experience in supporting or leading engagement with First Nations stakeholders.
•	Tertiary qualifications in relevant field. 
•	Knowledge of AICD’s role as the nation’s leading governance education and membership body.
•	Computer literate including office suite and media/stakeholder systems.

Competencies                                                       
•	Entrepreneurial and Commercial Thinking
•	Presenting and Communicating Information
•	Planning and Organising
•	Adapting and Responding to Change
•	Writing and Reporting 
•	Self-Motivation and Moral Courage

Role purpose
To lead, develop and execute the AICD’s ASX Sector Strategy with the vision to increase the impact, reach, engagement and relevance of the AICD’s work with ASX directors and organisations. The ASX Lead will establish and drive systems to ensure that the AICD’s work is informed through authentic and effective engagement with key stakeholders. The role will drive improved harmonisation and coordination of AICD’s existing work across all business units, support the development of partnerships and new programs, manage a formal consultative forum as well as deliver targeted initiatives.   

Key responsibilities 
ASX Sector Strategy
•	Lead development of the AICD’s overarching ASX Sector Strategy, including principles and strategic priorities for AICD engagement and partnerships.
•	Establish and maintain internal processes to promote coordination, engagement and visibility of agreed strategic priorities across the AICD’s departments and divisions.
•	Develop effective internal reporting and planning mechanisms to support strategic priorities. 
•	Represent the AICD in strategy consultation and development. 

Partnership and Engagement
•	Lead collaboration on principles for AICD engagement and partnerships.
•	Cultivate key relationships within the ASX sector, including directors, organisations, governance and education bodies, regulators, peak and representational bodies and government.
•	Engage with the ASX Chairs Forum to seek input and endorsement of the strategy. 
•	Negotiate agreements and support the establishment of long-term and strategic partnerships, both internal and external, to increase the impact of AICD offerings and presence.
•	Collaborate with the AICD Senior Leadership team to develop and implement the AICD strategy.
•	Meaningfully engage with division councils and relevant advisory committees, fostering alignment to drive objectives. 

Representation
•	Represent the AICD at a senior level when attending or participating in forums, events, and engaging with government. 
•	In accordance with the AICD’s media policy, represent the AICD in media as required.
•	Regularly engage with ASX stakeholders to inform and facilitate the development and implementation of the AICD ASX Sector Strategy.
•	As appropriate with other kindred organisations to represent AICD and raise AICD’s profile.
•	Engage with event and program stakeholders, along with program clients, advocating the AICD’s ASX sector agenda. 

Impact and Relevance of AICD Offerings
•	In collaboration with the Engagement and the Education and Policy Leadership departments, develop and maintain systems to align AICD programs, partnerships and delivery with strategic priorities and outcomes (including measurement of impact, pricing considerations and partnership objectives).

AICD Contribution
•	Contribute to the development of the department operation plan, suggesting ideas that create solutions for the team, and in turn the organisation. 
•	Undertake or contribute to specific National and State projects or initiatives that support Australian Institute of Company Directors’ strategic plan as required. 
•	Proactively contribute feedback and ideas to Australian Institute of Company Directors for continuous improvement and to achieve OKRs, budgets and strategic goals. 

Other duties in line with competencies and skills may be required on an ad hoc or permanent basis

Essential knowledge, skills and experience
•	MBA or Degree qualified in Business or commercial / professional discipline. 10 years’ experience in senior role in or working with listed organisations. 
•	Proven ability to lead the development of impact strategies, linking global and societal trends in corporate governance with stakeholder perspectives and AICD values.
•	Demonstrated effective and authentic engagement with ASX stakeholders on governance issues.
•	Strong ability to inspire and influence others, fostering a shared sense of purpose and direction to achieve organisational objectives.
•	Demonstrated knowledge of international and national policies and issues impacting corporate governance and direct exposure of the ASX Corporate Governance Principles. 
•	Strong communication skills with the ability to present logical and compelling arguments.
•	Ability to prepare and execute high quality plans, briefs, submissions, correspondence and other written material.
•	Highly developed negotiation, facilitation and presentation skills at a leadership audience level. 
•	Capacity to work independently, flexibly and responsively to achieve results.
•	Strong project management skills and ability to manage competing priorities.
•	Proactive in anticipating stakeholder needs, demonstrating flexibility, collaboration, and initiative.

•	Working with people
•	Learning and researching
•	Formulating strategies and concepts
•	Persuading and influencing
•	Coping with pressure and setbacks

As a Supermarket Manager, your role will involve overseeing the day-to-day operations of a retail store. This will involve managing staff schedules, ensuring that inventory is properly stocked, and implementing pricing strategies to maximize profits. Additionally, you will be responsible for ensuring that safety protocols are followed, and that the store is clean and presentable for customers.

Retail expertise: A supermarket manager should have extensive knowledge and experience in retail operations, including inventory management, customer service, and merchandising.

	Sound knowledge of family violence and the impact on children and families

	Demonstrated ability to develop, implement and evaluate personal and relationship education

Faith Basis of Appointment We see the appointment of staff to Trinity Christian School as a ministry calling. The ethos, aims and objects of a Christian School are based on: > acceptance of the Lordship of Christ; and > acceptance of the Bible as the revealed Word of God Within Trinity Christian School these are expounded in the Doctrinal Basis. The Doctrinal Basis does not exhaust the extent of the beliefs of the School and its members. The Bible itself, as the inspired and infallible Word of God, that speaks with final authority concerning truth, morality, and the proper conduct of mankind, is the sole and final source of all that the School and its members believe. You must support the School in these areas and set an example of personal conduct and behaviour consistent with the School’s beliefs and ethos as outlined in documents made available to prospective employees prior to interview. Any appointment is conditional upon staff maintaining and demonstrating a continuing firm personal belief and lifestyle consistent with the School’s Doctrinal Basis and the other documents provided to staff, this being an essential condition of the appointment. A Learning Centred Christian School At Trinity Christian School we endeavour to make the link between our faith, what we do and how we teach. We call our students and school community to grow “loving hearts, enquiring minds and generous spirits”. At Trinity, how we teach must help our students in becoming who they were created to be in Christ. How we teach should also bring a Kingdom perspective, set students’ minds on things above, deliver meaning and show purpose. The pedagogy in our classrooms at Trinity should engage enquiring minds, build questioning, allow for grappling, exploring and reasoning. The students we teach should be given opportunities to grow and serve, so they may see who they are becoming and their purpose. As we undertake to grow our learners in both faith and learning, we must build in them the capacity to do so. As stated in our Strategic Priorities for 2024 – 2027, we have several key areas of focus that we are working toward: Christian Faith and Growth The spiritual nurturing of all individuals in the Christian faith, within a caring community of students, staff and families united by vision, mission and values. A passionate focus on realising the transformative power of the Gospel of Jesus, developing Biblical knowledge, and understanding and fulfilling the purpose of a Christian life. Excellence in Education and Learning All students actively engaged, growing and thriving as learners in a learning centred school. Parent partnership and student agency enhanced and supported. A commitment to educational excellence with the creation and support of new ways of learning, varied student pathways and models of education, to meet the needs of each learner. Community and Belonging A movement by all students, staff and families from joining and attending the school to belonging and actively investing in the Trinity community. Connection and relationship developed and strengthened with those in the School’s history as well as future community members. Connecting with the World and Shaping Tomorrow Ongoing design and formation of our 2030 learning environment for the community now and in the future. A growth in our reach and impact on Christian Education nationally and globally, including shaping a reputation as a centre for staffing development and excellence. Benefits of Working at Trinity Christian School Trinity Christian School is an employer that promotes staff excellence and values the benefit that our professional team bring to the school. In addition to the highly competitive remuneration and conditions as set out in the MEA we offer the following additional benefits to our staff across the school including: > Attractive salary packaging arrangements. > School fee concessions. > Generous annual Staff Development Award program. > Access to an Employee Assistance Program. > Access to annual influenza vaccinations. Other Essential Requirements Employment Conditions Employment will be subject to the conditions of the current ACT Christian School General Staff Multi-Enterprise Agreement 2021 or its replacement. The salary and classification for this position will be dependent on the qualifications and experience of the successful applicant. A Committed, Practicing Christian Applicants must be able to demonstrate a personal faith and commitment to the Lord Jesus Christ and a lifestyle consistent with that faith, including an active commitment to a Christian fellowship. Right to Work In Australia Applicants must be Australian Citizens or hold a current and valid working visa. The School will not consider sponsorship nor support a visa application process. Please do not submit an application if you cannot meet this requirement. About the Position The Public Intoxication Response (PIR) service plays a crucial role in addressing the health and wellbeing needs of Aboriginal and/or Torres Strait Islander individuals who are intoxicated in the Greater Bendigo/Castlemaine areas, aligning with the recent decriminalisation of public intoxication by the Victorian Government. The PIR Team Leader will hold a supporting position in this service, ensuring that the program operates effectively and efficiently. The core function of the PIR team is to provide an outreach-based response to individuals in need. This response includes conducting thorough health assessments, offering safe transportation to an appropriate location, monitoring the health and overall well-being of the individual, and delivering follow-up care as needed. In essence, the PIR service operates as a critical lifeline for those who are intoxicated, ensuring their safety and well-being while also contributing to a more compassionate and health-focused approach in the context of public intoxication. Key Responsibilities The Public Intoxication Response (PIR) Team Leader will have the following responsibilities: Contribution to PIR Model Development and Evaluation: This entails actively participating in the development of the PIR model, evaluating its effectiveness, and making necessary adjustments to ensure it aligns with the evolving needs of Community. Recruitment and Management: The PIR Team Leader will support the Program Manager in recruitment and day-to-day management of the PIR team, ensuring that they are well-trained, motivated, and capable of carrying out their roles effectively. Liaison With Other Internal BDAC Programs: Work with other areas of BDAC to facilitate referral pathways while integrating with the broader Health Wellbeing Team. Staff Rostering, Including On-Call Scheduling: Organising and managing staff rosters, which include on-call schedules to ensure that there is adequate coverage and response readiness. Client Wellbeing Oversight: The PIR Team Leader will provide oversight, offering guidance and support to the team in handling complex cases, ensuring adherence to protocols, and maintaining high standards of care. Reporting and Evaluation: The Team Leader will be responsible for collecting data, preparing reports, and evaluating the programs performance. Client Advocacy: The PIR Team Leader advocates for the rights and well-being of clients, ensuring that they receive appropriate care and support while navigating the complexities of the program Community and Stakeholder Engagement: Working with the Program Manager to build meaningful stakeholder relationships that will benefit Community using the service.

The Career Coach (CC) plays a crucial role in empowering individuals with disability, injury, or a health condition to achieve their employment goals under the new Inclusive Employment Australia (IEA) framework. This role involves providing information, support, and representation to participants to ensure their rights are upheld and they can participate fully and equally in the IEA program and the broader community. This role assists participants throughout their employment journey from initial contact and job preparation to securing and maintaining employment. Excellence in Education and Learning All students actively engaged, growing and thriving as learners in a learning centred school. Parent partnership and student agency enhanced and supported. A commitment to educational excellence with the creation and support of new ways of learning, varied student pathways and models of education, to meet the needs of each learner. Community and Belonging A movement by all students, staff and families from joining and attending the school to belonging and actively investing in the Trinity community. Connection and relationship developed and strengthened with those in the School’s history as well as future community members. Connecting with the World and Shaping Tomorrow Ongoing design and formation of our 2030 learning environment for the community now and in the future. A growth in our reach and impact on Christian Education nationally and globally, including shaping a reputation as a centre for staffing development and excellence. The successful applicant will work with Aboriginal men and younger males, as well as fathers of Aboriginal children, coming through The Orange Door (TOD) for support around family violence and child wellbeing. The role will work to support men to engage with services, including working collaboratively to transition men into programs run through BDAC, most commonly with the mens practitioners in the Family Safety and Healing Program, as well as Family Services, AOD and SEWB, amongst others. The successful applicant will develop strong relationships across the service system in the Dja Dja Wurrung region to support the best service connection for men coming through TOD. Key Responsibilities Providing culturally safe and responsive support to Aboriginal men, young males and fathers/carers of Aboriginal children by: Co-working and providing daily specialist practice support for the Hubs staff Managing a caseload of complex and/or sensitive cases Operating with autonomy and accountability in supporting Aboriginal men, young people and fathers/carers of Aboriginal children. Working directly with the Mens Practitioners in the BDAC Family Safety and Healing Program to promote effective transition from TOD to ongoing support for people who use violence (PUV), young males and fathers of Aboriginal Children needing family support. Mentoring and developing Hub Staff in working with Aboriginal men, young males and fathers/carers of Aboriginal children: Building capability of Hub practitioners to deliver culturally competent responses to Aboriginal children, women and men, informed by cultural expertise, client experience and in line with the relevant practice frameworks and legislation Providing practitioners with relevant information and access to systems to support safe and effective responses to Aboriginal children and families as part of an integrated practice approach Modelling integrated practice approaches and behaviours integral to ethical clinical practice working with Aboriginal children and families Sharing practice knowledge on Aboriginal approaches to holistic healing and whole of family practices Building the cultural safety of the Hub and supporting choice and self-determination of Aboriginal people. Liaising with organisations and services within the Hub network in order to improve direct service issues for Aboriginal people accessing services through the Hub. Keep accurate and complete records of your work activities in accordance with legislative requirements and the Victoria Governments, information security and privacy policies and requirements. Take reasonable care for your own health and safety and for that of others in the workplace by working in accordance with legislative requirements and occupational health and safety (OHS) policies and procedures. Other duties as directed by the Aboriginal Practice Leader and consistent with the overall goals and responsibilities of this position

A Principal is responsible for managing a school or educational institution. They develop and implement academic and disciplinary policies, supervise and evaluate teaching staff, oversee student programs, and manage school budgets. Principals must be experienced educators with excellent leadership, communication, and organizational skills. They should be able to create a positive learning environment and promote a culture of excellence among students and staff.

Leadership: Demonstrated ability to lead and manage a team of teachers and staff, set goals and priorities, and create a positive school culture. Communication: Excellent communication skills to effectively communicate with students, parents, teachers, and other stakeholders. Curriculum and Instruction: In-depth understanding of curriculum and instruction in subject/grade level, as well as the ability to stay up-to-date with new trends and best practices. School Administration: Knowledge of school administration, including budgeting, hiring, and policy development. Decision-making: The ability to make sound decisions based on data and analysis. Problem-solving: The ability to identify and solve complex problems that may arise in the school. Interpersonal skills: The ability to build relationships and work collaboratively with teachers, staff, parents, and community members. Education and Certification: Masters degree in education or a related field, relevant teaching and administrative experience, and state-issued administrative certification. Knowledge of Laws and Regulations: Knowledge of federal and state laws and regulations related to education, including student safety, teacher certification, and testing requirements. Visionary: A visionary leader who can motivate and inspire their team and develop a clear vision for the schools future.

FUNCTIONS OF THE ROLE The Administration & Facilities Manager is responsible for overseeing the organisation’s administrative operations and facilities management to ensure the effective, safe, and reliable delivery of services. This role provides leadership and coordination of office administration, systems, and infrastructure, as well as the maintenance function responsible for supporting client-facing services and organisational assets. The position ensures facilities, environments, and operational supports are fit for purpose, compliant, and aligned with organisational needs, enabling frontline teams to deliver high‑quality services to clients. ROLES AND RESPONSIBILITIES Organisation Works within the role and responsibilities for the position and complies with all organisational policies, regulations and standards, and Code of Conduct; Maintains professional and technical knowledge by participating in training and development activities; Represents the organisation as the first point of contact via a range of communication methods; Contributes effectively to team effort by accomplishing related results as needed; Always maintains a high level of professionalism. Duties and Tasks -Administration Oversees the administration support to a variety of areas within the organisation; When necessary, performs reception duties, including telephone enquiries, welcoming visitors and conducting frontline communications in a professional and courteous manner; Assists with booking venues and meeting rooms (internal and external) and receiving visitors to the office; Oversees the development and production of the newsletter, InfoCast's, flyers, posters, and other media; Monitors shared inbox and deals with incoming mail accordingly; Coordinates events management and catering for in-house and external events; Conducts financial transactions for clients as directed including electronic payments and receipt of cash transactions; Orders stationary and supplies as required; Undertakes routine administration duties including: Mail collection and distribution; Word processing and spreadsheet activities; Photocopying and scanning; Ensuring a professionally presented office; Provide relief at other sites when required; Records maintenance and archiving; Ensures the Administration Team keeps the bathrooms and kitchens stocked and in order. Ensures discretion and confidentiality of information/documents is maintained at all times; Assists with mailouts and other company correspondence. Duties and Tasks – Facilities Oversees the organisation’s facilities and maintenance services to ensure environments are safe, functional, compliant, and fit for purpose for clients, team members, and visitors. Manages and supervises Maintenance Officers, providing direction, support, performance oversight, and prioritisation of work activities. Coordinates and manages maintenance services that directly support client ‑ facing service delivery, including service sites, client environments, and organisational assets. Ensures timely response to maintenance requests and issues across service locations, prioritising matters that impact client safety, dignity, and continuity of services. Liaises with external contractors and service providers to coordinate repairs, servicing, and maintenance works, ensuring quality outcomes and value for money. Oversees planned and reactive maintenance programs to maintain asset condition and minimise disruption to services provided to clients. Monitors facilities and maintenance compliance with relevant legislation, safety standards, and organisational policies and procedures. Works collaboratively with service teams to ensure facilities and maintenance activities align with client needs and service delivery requirements. Maintains accurate records of maintenance activities, inspections, servicing schedules, and contractor documentation. Assists with facilities planning and minor projects, including office moves, site set ‑ ups, refurbishments, and equipment replacement. Supports incident management processes by addressing facilities or maintenance issues arising from incidents, hazards, audits, or client feedback. Promotes a proactive approach to facilities and maintenance that supports safety, service quality, and positive client experiences. Team Members’ Supervision Provides support, guidance, and direction to the teams; Ensures that all team members demonstrate appropriate behaviours when working with clients, colleagues, volunteers, and the public; Leads and promotes effective teamwork and communication between all team members; Identifies and implements team members’ training and development needs; Regularly reviews and maintains effective performance management processes; Builds workforce capability by providing ongoing informal and formal performance development and performance management to direct reports; Works alongside the team, setting the example with a proactive attitude and strong leadership skills; Oversees the recruitment, selection, orientation and on-boarding procedures of team members; Achieves results by communicating position roles, responsibilities, accountabilities and expectations to team members; Performs coaching, counselling and disciplinary procedures for team members with the support of the Head of Operations. Other Ensures a strong customer service focus through responsive, effective customer engagement; Contributes effectively to organisational events and activities. KEY RELATIONSHIPS Head of Operations Administration Team Facilities Team Management team Team Members, Clients and Stakeholders

About the Position The Wurrekangek (Aboriginal Family Preservation and Reunification Response) program is funded by the Victorian Department of Families, Fairness and Housing (DFFH). In collaboration with VACCA, the Wurrekangek Program delivers an immediate, intensive, and culturally appropriate response to Aboriginal and/or Torres Strait Islander families who are at risk of having their children removed from their care or are in the Reunification phase of their involvement with Child Protection. The core values of the program include supporting self-determination, self-advocacy and strengthening families through evidence based and culturally safe practice. Key Responsibilities The Wurrekangek (AFPR Response) Practitioner will engage with Aboriginal and/or Torres Strait Islander families in a culturally safe and competent way. This includes engaging in regular consultation with the programs Team Leader/ Cultural Advisor and ongoing training. The Wurrekangek (AFPR Response) Practitioner will: Demonstrate ability to work with Aboriginal children, young people, and families with complex trauma needs. Have sound understanding of the history of colonisation and resulting impacts on Aboriginal and/or Torres Strait Islander families. Work with families to ensure their cultural needs are identified early and embedded in practice. Work alongside of families to identify their strengths, barriers and needs. Implement a holistic case planning model using the cultural and evidence informed AFPR practice elements. Maintain clear and concise documentation of case progression and implementation of evidence-based interventions. Participate in all training, supervision, and consultation activities. Build strong relationships with community stakeholders and other culturally appropriate organisations.
